medications.drug_name: large 'catch-up' insulin dose administered at home
medications.indication_rationale: Insulin correction in DKA must be carefully titrated intravenously alongside fluid resuscitation and potassium monitoring in hospital; an uncontrolled home dose risks dangerous hypokalaemia and cardiac arrhythmia.
contraindications_in_this_case: Suspected diabetic ketoacidosis with vomiting and dehydration

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the patient lying down and monitor consciousness closely while awaiting the ambulance; do not attempt oral rehydration if vomiting continues.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Maintains safety and prepares for emergency assessment; oral rehydration is unlikely to be tolerated and may worsen vomiting.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like diabetic ketoacidosis — missing insulin doses during your illness has led to a dangerous build-up of acid in your blood, alongside very high blood sugar and dehydration.
Call 000 for an ambulance right now — you need IV fluids and a carefully monitored insulin drip in hospital, not a large insulin dose at home.
Try to stay lying down and calm while waiting for the ambulance.
Bring your insulin pens and blood sugar logbook with you so the hospital team knows your recent doses.

follow_up_plan.interval: Immediate emergency transport and hospital admission for IV fluids and insulin infusion
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Electrolyte correction, resolution of acidosis, identification/treatment of the precipitating illness, and endocrinology review for sick-day management planning
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Not applicable — already an emergency requiring immediate transport

must_include_items: Explicit advice against giving a large home insulin dose
Recognition that this is DKA, not simple gastroenteritis
Advice to bring insulin pens/logbook to hospital
errors_of_omission: No warning against home insulin correction
errors_of_commission: Recommending a large catch-up insulin dose at home
Suggesting oral rehydration as sufficient management
Treating this as simple gastroenteritis with a routine GP follow-up
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Recommending home insulin correction instead of emergency transport is a critical, potentially fatal safety failure.
